case_name: UNITED INDIA INSURANCE CO. LTD., vs SMT PARVATHI HEBBAR, W/O LATE RAMAKRISHNA HEBBAR
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru

compensation_breakdown.interest: 6% p.a. from date of petition till payment
compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal award of Rs.2,45,000/- confirmed; High Court modified liability but directed insurer to pay and recover.

facts: On 02-07-2008 at about 6:30 a.m. the deceased Ramakrishna Hebbar was struck by a Maruthi Omni (Reg. No. KA-20/Z-3931) while standing on the southern side mud portion of Udupi-Karkala Road in front of Manipal Bakery, Shivalli village, Udupi. He was shifted to KMC Hospital, Manipal and succumbed to injuries on 16-07-2008. The claimants (wife and children) filed a petition claiming Rs.11,48,000/- alleging rash and negligent driving; the offending Omni was insured with United India Insurance Co. Ltd. The insurer disputed liability mainly on the ground that the drivers driving license had expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed only on 04-07-2008.
arguments_claimant: The claimants argued that the accident occurred due to rash and negligent driving of the Maruthi Omni and the vehicle was insured with the appellant insurer, entitling them to compensation. They contended that subsequent renewal of the driver's license and the driver's long history of holding licenses (since 1992) meant there was no breach of policy conditions and relied on precedents (K G Srinivasamurthy; National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh) to support insurer liability.
arguments_respondent: The insurer contended the driver did not possess a valid and effective driving license on the date of the accident because the license expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed only on 04-07-2008, more than 30 days after expiry. Relying on Ram Babu Tiwari and other Supreme Court decisions, the insurer argued that where renewal is applied for after 30 days, the renewed license takes effect from the date of renewal and insurer is not liable to indemnify; insurer also pleaded contributory negligence by the deceased (though not proved).
ratio_decidendi: The court decided the narrow legal question of whether the insurer was liable where the drivers license had expired prior to the accident and was renewed after the expiry. The court applied the statutory rule in Section 15 (and the proviso) that "where an application for the renewal of a license is made more than thirty days after the date of its expiry, the driving license shall be renewed with effect from the date of its renewal," and quoted the Supreme Court in Ram Babu Tiwari: "It is beyond any doubt or dispute that only in the event an application for renewal of license is filed within a period 30 days from the date of expiry thereof, the same would be renewed automatically which means that even if an accident had taken place within the aforementioned period, the driver may be held to be possessing a valid license." The court held that "subsequent renewal will come into operation only from the date of renewal and not from the retrospective period" and therefore the driver did not hold an effective license at the time of the accident. However, balancing the statutory right of the third party and the benevolent object of the Act, the court applied the "pay and recover" principle directing the insurer to pay the awarded compensation and then recover it from the insured/driver.
final_order: The appeal is allowed in part and the Tribunals award dated 18-03-2011 is modified: liability is fastened on the owner of the Maruthi Omni, but the Insurance Company is directed to pay the awarded sum to the claimants and then to recover the said sum from the insured in the same proceedings by applying the principle of "Pay and Recover."
summary: This is a 2017 Karnataka High Court motor-accident appeal by United India Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.2,45,000/- to the family of Ramakrishna Hebbar who died after being hit by a Maruthi Omni on 02-07-2008. The key legal issue was whether the insurer is liable where the drivers license had expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed on 04-07-2008 (more than 30 days later). The High Court held the driver lacked an effective licence at the accident time and placed legal liability on the owner, but directed the insurer to pay the award and "pay and recover" from the insured, so the claimants receive compensation.
embedding_summary: United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt Parvathi Hebbar & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, MFA.NO.4616/2011 (MV), judgment dated 20 June 2017. Accident on 02-07-2008 at Manipal (Shivalli, Udupi): Maruthi Omni (Reg. KA-20/Z-3931) struck Ramakrishna Hebbar who died on 16-07-2008. Key statutes cited: Section 15 M.V. Act (proviso re: 30-day renewal), Section 3, Section 5, Section 14, Section 149(2)(a)(ii), and Rule 3 CMVR; procedural appeal u/s 173(1) MV Act. Core issues: expired driving license (expiry 30-04-2008; renewal 04-07-2008), whether renewal after 30 days confers retrospective validity, insurers liability to indemnify, and application of "pay and recover". Court relied on Ram Babu Tiwari (2008 ACJ 2654), National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh, National Insurance v. Vidhyadhar Mahariwala, Kusum Rai, Ishwar Chandra and K G Srinivasamurthy and MANUARA KHATUN v. RAJESH Kr. SINGH. Outcome: Tribunal award of Rs.2,45,000/- (with interest @6% p.a. from petition) upheld for payment; liability formally fastened on owner but insurer directed to "pay and recover."
legal_principles: Where renewal of driving licence is applied for more than 30 days after expiry, renewal takes effect from the date of renewal only (Section 15 proviso).
Subsequent renewal after the 30-day period does not confer retrospective validity for accidents occurring in the interregnum.
Holding of a valid and effective driving licence is a condition of the insurance contract; absence disentitles insurer from indemnity in principle.
Owner is responsible if vehicle is entrusted to a person not holding a valid licence (duty under Section 5/Sections 3-4).
Court can apply the "pay and recover" doctrine to secure compensation for third parties despite insurer/owner policy breaches.
Insurer who establishes defense under Section 149(2)(a)(ii) may still be directed to pay and later recover the amount where justice and the benevolent object of the Act require.
cases_cited: Ram Babu Tiwari -vs- United India Insurance Co. Ltd. and others, 2008 ACJ 2654
National Insurance Co. Ltd. -vs- Swaran Singh, (2004) 3 SCC 297
National Insurance Co. Ltd. -vs- Vidhyadhar Mahariwala, 2008 ACJ 2860
National Insurance Co. Ltd. -vs- Kusum Rai, 2006 ACJ 1336 (SC)
Ishwar Chandra -vs- Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd., 2007 ACJ 1067 (SC)
K G Srinivasamurthy and others -vs- Smt. Habib Khathun and others, ILR 2002 Kar 1088
MANUARA KHATUN AND OTHERS v. RAJESH Kr. SINGH AND OTHERS, AIR 2017 SCW 1204

LTD., vs SMT PARVATHI HEBBAR, W/O LATE RAMAKRISHNA HEBBAR High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru 2017 On 02-07-2008 at about 6:30 a.m. the deceased Ramakrishna Hebbar was struck by a Maruthi Omni (Reg. No. KA-20/Z-3931) while standing on the southern side mud portion of Udupi-Karkala Road in front of Manipal Bakery, Shivalli village, Udupi. He was shifted to KMC Hospital, Manipal and succumbed to injuries on 16-07-2008. The claimants (wife and children) filed a petition claiming Rs.11,48,000/- alleging rash and negligent driving; the offending Omni was insured with United India Insurance Co. Ltd. The insurer disputed liability mainly on the ground that the drivers driving license had expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed only on 04-07-2008. The claimants argued that the accident occurred due to rash and negligent driving of the Maruthi Omni and the vehicle was insured with the appellant insurer, entitling them to compensation. They contended that subsequent renewal of the driver's license and the driver's long history of holding licenses (since 1992) meant there was no breach of policy conditions and relied on precedents (K G Srinivasamurthy; National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh) to support insurer liability. The insurer contended the driver did not possess a valid and effective driving license on the date of the accident because the license expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed only on 04-07-2008, more than 30 days after expiry. Relying on Ram Babu Tiwari and other Supreme Court decisions, the insurer argued that where renewal is applied for after 30 days, the renewed license takes effect from the date of renewal and insurer is not liable to indemnify; insurer also pleaded contributory negligence by the deceased (though not proved). The court decided the narrow legal question of whether the insurer was liable where the drivers license had expired prior to the accident and was renewed after the expiry. The court applied the statutory rule in Section 15 (and the proviso) that "where an application for the renewal of a license is made more than thirty days after the date of its expiry, the driving license shall be renewed with effect from the date of its renewal," and quoted the Supreme Court in Ram Babu Tiwari: "It is beyond any doubt or dispute that only in the event an application for renewal of license is filed within a period 30 days from the date of expiry thereof, the same would be renewed automatically which means that even if an accident had taken place within the aforementioned period, the driver may be held to be possessing a valid license." The court held that "subsequent renewal will come into operation only from the date of renewal and not from the retrospective period" and therefore the driver did not hold an effective license at the time of the accident. However, balancing the statutory right of the third party and the benevolent object of the Act, the court applied the "pay and recover" principle directing the insurer to pay the awarded compensation and then recover it from the insured/driver. The appeal is allowed in part and the Tribunals award dated 18-03-2011 is modified: liability is fastened on the owner of the Maruthi Omni, but the Insurance Company is directed to pay the awarded sum to the claimants and then to recover the said sum from the insured in the same proceedings by applying the principle of "Pay and Recover." This is a 2017 Karnataka High Court motor-accident appeal by United India Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.2,45,000/- to the family of Ramakrishna Hebbar who died after being hit by a Maruthi Omni on 02-07-2008. The key legal issue was whether the insurer is liable where the drivers license had expired on 30-04-2008 and was renewed on 04-07-2008 (more than 30 days later). The High Court held the driver lacked an effective licence at the accident time and placed legal liability on the owner, but directed the insurer to pay the award and "pay and recover" from the insured, so the claimants receive compensation. United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt Parvathi Hebbar & Ors., High Court of Karnataka, MFA.NO.4616/2011 (MV), judgment dated 20 June 2017. Accident on 02-07-2008 at Manipal (Shivalli, Udupi): Maruthi Omni (Reg. KA-20/Z-3931) struck Ramakrishna Hebbar who died on 16-07-2008. Key statutes cited: Section 15 M.V. Act (proviso re: 30-day renewal), Section 3, Section 5, Section 14, Section 149(2)(a)(ii), and Rule 3 CMVR; procedural appeal u/s 173(1) MV Act. Core issues: expired driving license (expiry 30-04-2008; renewal 04-07-2008), whether renewal after 30 days confers retrospective validity, insurers liability to indemnify, and application of "pay and recover". Court relied on Ram Babu Tiwari (2008 ACJ 2654), National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh, National Insurance v. Vidhyadhar Mahariwala, Kusum Rai, Ishwar Chandra and K G Srinivasamurthy and MANUARA KHATUN v. RAJESH Kr. SINGH. Outcome: Tribunal award of Rs.2,45,000/- (with interest @6% p.a. from petition) upheld for payment; liability formally fastened on owner but insurer directed to "pay and recover." Ramakrishna Hebbar (deceased) Rs.2,45,000/- Rs.2,45,000/- 6% p.a. from date of petition till payment Tribunal award of Rs.2,45,000/- confirmed; High Court modified liability but directed insurer to pay and recover. Where renewal of driving licence is applied for more than 30 days after expiry, renewal takes effect from the date of renewal only (Section 15 proviso). Subsequent renewal after the 30-day period does not confer retrospective validity for accidents occurring in the interregnum. Holding of a valid and effective driving licence is a condition of the insurance contract; absence disentitles insurer from indemnity in principle. Owner is responsible if vehicle is entrusted to a person not holding a valid licence (duty under Section 5/Sections 3-4). Court can apply the "pay and recover" doctrine to secure compensation for third parties despite insurer/owner policy breaches. Insurer who establishes defense under Section 149(2)(a)(ii) may still be directed to pay and later recover the amount where justice and the benevolent object of the Act require.
